items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения... У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation: Once again I’m plagued by vague doubts... Shpak has a tape recorder, the ambassador has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.justification: The opening clause preserves the mock-heroic tone sufficiently, but the rendering of 'У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон' as 'Shpak has a tape recorder, the ambassador has a medallion' incorrectly expresses present possession. The reference (and analysis) indicates these items are the objects of suspicion (each had lost the corresponding item), not that they currently possess them. This is a semantic/accuracy error that changes the meaning and the comedic callback.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation: Hello, Your Majesty, very pleased. Your Majesty, very pleased, Your Majesty. Very pleased, hello, Your Majesty.
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.justification: The approved analysis stresses that Bunsha is absurdly INTRODUCING himself as 'the Tsar' (self-identification), which is central to the joke. The actual translation turns these lines into addresses to a monarch ('Your Majesty'), changing the fundamental comedic mechanic (self-introduction vs. addressing). This shifts register and intention and therefore fails to preserve the reference's core comic effect.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.justification: Identical in meaning and register to the reference. The line captures Bunsha's sudden imperial demand and preserves the comedic subversion of his usual character. Because the actual translation matches the reference, it is treated as perfect.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I’m in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.justification: The translation matches the reference in register and meaning (archaic, high-register dismissal). It preserves the Tsar's melancholic, regal tone. As it is effectively identical to the reference, no issues are recorded.

items.translation: They’ve bricked me in! Bricked me in, you demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.justification: The reference analysis emphasizes that 'demons' are the SUBJECT (i.e., the Tsar perceives the action as done by demons), not an addressee. The actual translation turns 'демоны' into a vocative ('you demons!'), changing the grammatical role and the tone: from a panicked attribution of causation to a shouted insult. This is a meaning-altering error that undermines the intended comic image of a confused, awe-struck autocrat interpreting technology as sorcery.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.justification: Both versions identify the cross as the cause of the 'miracle,' but the reference's 'Behold the power...' has a more absolute, archaic, and triumphant register, strengthening the Tsar's conviction that faith affected a mechanical device. The actual 'that's what... can do' softens that conviction and reduces the mock-heroic tone. This is a stylistic degradation of the intended comedic effect.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.justification: The actual translation communicates the same comic overturning of decorum: an invitation to collective, uninhibited dancing. Although wording differs (imperative 'Everybody dance!' versus invitation 'The floor is open to everyone!'), the effect—collapse of historical restraint into mass dancing—is preserved. Per instructions, different wording that achieves the same creative effect is acceptable.

items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.justification: Both translations convey Miloslavsky's pleased, opportunistic reaction on entering and discovering valuable goods. The actual line does not imply he is leaving (it correctly treats the entry as fortuitous) and preserves the casual, cheerful tone from the reference. The creative effect is maintained, so no issue.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation: Black caviar, red caviar… Yes! Exotic caviar… eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.justification: The approved analysis warns against translating the final item as 'caviar' because that would confuse viewers: the joke depends on presenting 'eggplant' as a cheap Soviet spread contrasted with real caviar. The actual translation's phrase 'Exotic caviar… eggplant!' effectively labels the eggplant as 'exotic caviar,' which risks muddling the joke and misrepresents the intended satirical inversion. This is a meaning-sensitive error and undermines the satire.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.justification: The actual translation matches the reference wording and order, preserving the comedic enumeration and the escalating, slightly forgetful recounting that makes the joke work. As it is effectively identical to the reference, it is treated as perfect.

items.original: И тебя вылечат! И тебя тоже вылечат! И меня вылечат!
items.translation: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.justification: The translation reproduces the calm, resigned, melodic repetition in the reference and retains the darkly comic acceptance of collective 'curing.' It matches the reference and preserves the intended effect, so no issues are raised.

summary.interpretation: 2.36 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.47 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
